title: ukbflow
text: ukbflow 提供了一套完整的、RAP 原生的 UK Biobank 分析工作流 —— 从表型提取、疾病衍生，到关联分析和发表级图表，全程在 RAP 云端环境中运行。 UK Biobank 数据政策（2024+）：个体水平数据必须保留在 RAP 环境中，不得下载到本地。所有 ukbflow 函数均遵循此约束设计。
code: library(ukbflow)  # 认证并提取数据 auth_login() df <- extract_pheno(c(31, 21022, 53, 20116, 41270, 41280)) |>   decode_values() |>   decode_names()  # 衍生疾病表型 df <- df |>   derive_missing() |>   derive_icd10(name = "outcome", icd10 = "E11",                source = c("hes", "first_occurrence")) |>   derive_followup(name = "outcome", event_col = "outcome_date",                   baseline_col = "date_baseline",                   censor_date  = as.Date("2022-06-01"))  # 关联分析 → 森林图 res <- assoc_coxph(df, outcome_col = "outcome_status",                    time_col = "outcome_followup_years",                    exposure_col = "exposure_status",                    covariates = c("age_at_recruitment", "sex", "tdi")) plot_forest(res)

title: ukbflow
text: 环境要求： R ≥ 4.1 · dxpy（dx-toolkit，RAP 交互必需）
code: # 推荐 pak::pkg_install("evanbio/ukbflow")  # 或者 remotes::install_github("evanbio/ukbflow") pip install dxpy

title: ukbflow
text: auth_login()、auth_status()、auth_select_project() — RAP 认证 fetch_ls()、fetch_tree()、fetch_url()、fetch_file() — RAP 文件系统 fetch_metadata()、fetch_field() — UKB 元数据快捷下载 extract_pheno()、extract_batch() — 表型提取 decode_values() — 整数编码 → 可读标签 decode_names() — 字段 ID → snake_case 列名 derive_missing() — 处理”不知道”/“不愿回答” derive_covariate() — 类型转换 + 分布汇总 derive_cut() — 连续变量分组 derive_selfreport() — 自报告疾病状态 + 日期 derive_hes() — HES 住院 ICD-10 derive_first_occurrence() — First Occurrence 字段 derive_cancer_registry() — 癌症注册 derive_death_registry() — 死亡注册 derive_icd10() — 多源合并（封装函数） derive_case() — 自报告 + ICD-10 最终合并 derive_timing() — 患病时间分类（现患 vs. 新发） derive_age() — 事件发生年龄 derive_followup() — 随访终止日期与随访年数 assoc_coxph() / assoc_cox() — Cox 比例风险模型（HR） assoc_logistic() / assoc_logit() — 逻辑回归（） assoc_linear() / assoc_lm() — 线性回归（β） assoc_coxph_zph() — 比例风险假设检验 assoc_subgroup() — 分层分析 + 交互项 LRT assoc_trend() — 剂量-反应趋势 + p_trend assoc_competing() — Fine-Gray 竞争风险（SHR） plot_forest() — 森林图（PNG / PDF / JPG / TIFF，300 dpi） plot_tableone() — 基线特征表（DOCX / HTML / PDF / PNG） grs_check() — 验证 SNP 权重文件 grs_bgen2pgen() — 在 RAP 上将 BGEN 转换为 PGEN（提交云端任务） grs_score() — 使用 plink2 跨染色体计算 GRS grs_standardize() / grs_zscore() — Z 分标准化 grs_validate() — /HR per SD、高 vs. 低、趋势检验、AUC/C-index

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_* functions fit regression models exposure variable return tidy result tables suitable downstream forest plots publication tables. Prerequisite: analysis dataset already contain derived case status, follow-time, covariates produced derive_* functions. See vignette("derive") vignette("derive-survival").

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: main functions automatically produce three adjustment levels without requiring manual formula construction: Age sex columns located automatically via UKB field cache (populated extract_pheno()). Set base = FALSE skip first two models run Fully adjusted model.

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_coxph() primary function time--event outcomes. accepts logical (TRUE/FALSE) integer (0/1) event indicators returns one row per exposure x term x model combination. Output columns:
code: library(ukbflow)  # Crude + age-sex adjusted (automatic) res <- assoc_coxph(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   time_col     = "outcome_followup_years",   exposure_col = c("exposure", "bmi_category") ) #> ── assoc_coxph ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ 2 exposures x 2 models = 4 Cox regressions #> ── exposure ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>   ✔ Unadjusted       | exposure: HR 1.43 (1.28-1.60), p < 0.001 #>   ✔ Age and sex adj  | exposure: HR 1.38 (1.23-1.55), p < 0.001 # Add a Fully adjusted model res <- assoc_coxph(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   time_col     = "outcome_followup_years",   exposure_col = "exposure",   covariates   = c("tdi", "smoking_status_i0", paste0("pc", 1:10)) )

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_logistic() binary outcomes without time dimension (e.g. case-control cross-sectional designs). sparse data small samples, use profile likelihood confidence intervals: Output identical structure assoc_coxph() OR_label place HR / HR_label, n_cases instead n_events / person_years.
code: res <- assoc_logistic(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   exposure_col = c("exposure", "bmi_category"),   covariates   = c("tdi", "smoking_status_i0", paste0("pc", 1:10)) ) res <- assoc_logistic(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   exposure_col = "exposure",   ci_method    = "profile"   # slower but more accurate for sparse data )

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_linear() continuous outcomes (e.g. biomarker levels, BMI). standard error beta included support downstream meta-analysis. Passing binary (0/1) column outcome_col triggers warning suggesting assoc_logistic() instead.
code: res <- assoc_linear(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "bmi",   exposure_col = c("exposure", "smoking_pack_years"),   covariates   = c("tdi", "alcohol_freq", paste0("pc", 1:10)) )

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_coxph_zph() re-fits Cox models tests PH assumption via Schoenfeld residuals (cox.zph()). Use alongside assoc_coxph() validate model assumptions. Output columns include chisq, df, p_value, ph_satisfied (logical), global test statistics (global_chisq, global_df, global_p). PH assumption violated, consider adding strata() assoc_coxph() modelling time-varying effects.
code: zph <- assoc_coxph_zph(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   time_col     = "outcome_followup_years",   exposure_col = c("exposure", "bmi_category"),   covariates   = c("tdi", "smoking_status_i0") ) #>   ✔ Unadjusted | exposure: chisq = 0.412, p = 0.521 [OK] satisfied #>   ✔ Unadjusted | bmi_category: chisq = 1.834, p = 0.176 [OK] satisfied  # Identify any violations zph[ph_satisfied == FALSE]

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_subgroup() stratifies dataset grouping variable fits specified model within subgroup. likelihood ratio test (LRT) exposure x subgroup interaction computed full dataset appended p_interaction. Output columns include subgroup, subgroup_level, p_interaction addition standard effect estimate columns. Note: variable automatically excluded subgroup models. include covariates — collinearity warning issued .
code: res <- assoc_subgroup(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   time_col     = "outcome_followup_years",   exposure_col = "exposure",   by           = "sex",   method       = "coxph",   covariates   = c("age_at_recruitment", "tdi", "smoking_status_i0") ) #> ── assoc_subgroup ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ 1 exposure x 2 models x 2 subgroups (sex) #> ℹ Computing interaction LRT (exposure x sex) on full data ... #>   ℹ Unadjusted | exposure: p_interaction = 0.214

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_trend() fits categorical trend models simultaneously ordered-factor exposure, returning per-category estimates alongside p-value linear trend. Supply custom scores category midpoints carry meaningful units: output contains reference row (HR = 1.00 (ref)) followed non-reference rows, HR_per_score, HR_per_score_label, p_trend appended shared columns.
code: # Create an ordered factor (e.g. from derive_cut()) cohort[, exposure_cat := factor(exposure_score,                                  levels = c(0, 1, 2),                                  labels = c("Low", "Medium", "High"),                                  ordered = TRUE)] res <- assoc_trend(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   time_col     = "outcome_followup_years",   exposure_col = "exposure_cat",   method       = "coxph",   covariates   = c("age_at_recruitment", "sex", "tdi") ) #>   ℹ Levels: Low -> Medium -> High | Scores: 0, 1, 2 #>   ℹ Unadjusted | exposure_catLow: 1.00 (ref) #>   ✔ Unadjusted | exposure_catMedium: HR 1.21 (1.08-1.36), p = 0.001 #>   ✔ Unadjusted | exposure_catHigh:   HR 1.54 (1.38-1.72), p < 0.001 #>   ℹ Unadjusted | trend: HR_per_score = 1.24 (1.14-1.35), p_trend < 0.001 res <- assoc_trend(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   time_col     = "outcome_followup_years",   exposure_col = "exposure_cat",   method       = "coxph",   scores       = c(0, 5, 14)   # e.g. median years per category )

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_competing() fits Fine-Gray subdistribution hazard model via survival::finegray() + inverse-probability--censoring-weighted Cox regression. Use competing event (e.g. death) can preclude outcome interest. Two input modes supported: Mode — single column encodes event types: Mode B — separate 0/1 columns primary competing events: Output uses SHR (subdistribution hazard ratio) SHR_label place HR, adds n_compete (number competing events analysis set).
code: res <- assoc_competing(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "censoring_type",   # 0 = censored, 1 = event, 2 = competing   time_col     = "followup_years",   exposure_col = "exposure",   event_val    = 1L,   compete_val  = 2L,   covariates   = c("tdi", "smoking_status_i0") ) res <- assoc_competing(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   # primary event   time_col     = "followup_years",   exposure_col = c("exposure", "bmi_category"),   compete_col  = "death_status",     # competing event   covariates   = c("tdi", "smoking_status_i0") )

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: functions return data.table feeds directly plot_forest():
code: library(ukbflow)  res <- assoc_coxph(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   time_col     = "outcome_followup_years",   exposure_col = "exposure",   covariates   = c("tdi", "smoking_status_i0") )  # Pass directly to plot_forest() plot_forest(res)  # Filter to a single model res[model == "Fully adjusted"]  # Export data.table::fwrite(res, "assoc_results.csv")

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: ukbflow interacts UK Biobank Research Analysis Platform (RAP) via DNAnexus dx toolkit. Authentication required access UKB project, submit jobs, retrieve results. Two working modes supported: Local → RAP: Run R locally, authenticate DNAnexus API token, interact RAP remotely. RAP → RAP: Run R inside RAP cloud environment (e.g., RStudio DNAnexus), authentication handled automatically platform.

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: Log DNAnexus platform Go Account Settings > API Tokens Click New Token, set appropriate expiry, copy token Keep token private. Treat like password — share commit version control.

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: recommended approach store token ~/.Renviron, local file never committed git. keeps token scripts entirely. restarting R, token available auth_login() automatically.
code: usethis::edit_r_environ() # Add the following line, then save and restart R: # DX_API_TOKEN=your_token_here

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: token cached locally dx toolkit persists across R sessions. need log — subsequent R sessions require re-authentication unless token expires explicitly log . prefer pass token directly (e.g., interactive session), can : Security note: Avoid saving auth_login("token") calls script files. Prefer .Renviron approach workflow intend save share.
code: library(ukbflow)  auth_login()  # reads DX_API_TOKEN from environment auth_login("your_token_here")

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: running inside RAP RStudio environment, authentication handled automatically platform. login step required:
code: library(ukbflow)  auth_status()  # verify the current session

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: auth_status() returns current user active project. Use confirm session running analysis.
code: auth_status() #> ✔ User:    user-XXXXXXXXXXXX #> ✔ Project: project-XXXXXXXXXXXX

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: returns RAP projects accessible account, along project IDs. Project IDs take form project-XXXXXXXXXXXX required auth_select_project().
code: auth_list_projects() #> project-XXXXXXXXXXXX : My UKB Project (CONTRIBUTOR) #> project-YYYYYYYYYYYY : Shared Analysis Project (VIEW)

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: ukbflow uses project IDs rather names avoid ambiguity. Use auth_list_projects() find correct ID first. selected, project context saved dx toolkit persists across sessions.
code: auth_select_project("project-XXXXXXXXXXXX") #> Project selected: project-XXXXXXXXXXXX

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: Logging clears local session cache. next call auth_* job_* function require re-authentication.
code: auth_logout() #> ✔ Logged out from DNAnexus.

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: DNAnexus API tokens limited validity period set creation time. token expires: auth_login() dx-dependent functions fail Generate new token DNAnexus platform log : update ~/.Renviron new token call auth_login() without arguments.
code: auth_login("your_new_token_here")

title: Authentication and Project Setup
code: library(ukbflow)  auth_login()                                 # authenticate auth_status()                                # verify session auth_list_projects()                         # find your project ID auth_select_project("project-XXXXXXXXXXXX")  # set active project  # ... run your analysis ...  auth_logout()                                # optional: clear session

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
text: Raw UKB phenotype data contains encoded column names values need converted analysis. outputs need decode_names() convert field ID column names human-readable snake_case. Call order matters: using extract_pheno() output, always run decode_values() decode_names(), value decoding relies numeric field ID still present column name.

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
code: library(ukbflow)  df <- extract_pheno(c(31, 54, 20116, 21022)) df <- decode_values(df)   # 0/1 → "Female"/"Male", etc. df <- decode_names(df)    # participant.p31 → sex

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
text: decode_values() converts raw integer codes human-readable labels categorical fields. Continuous, date, text fields left unchanged. requires two metadata files UKB Showcase. Download : point decode_values() directory (default matches fetch_metadata()):
code: df <- decode_values(df) #> ✔ Decoded 3 categorical columns; 2 non-categorical columns unchanged. fetch_metadata(dest_dir = "data/metadata/") df <- decode_values(df, metadata_dir = "data/metadata/")

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
text: Codes absent encoding table (including UKB missing codes -1, -3, -7) returned NA.

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
text: decode_names() renames columns field ID format snake_case labels using UKB field title dictionary.
code: df <- decode_names(df) #> ✔ Renamed 5 columns.

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
text: extract_pheno() format (participant.p31) extract_batch() format (p31) handled automatically.

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
text: UKB field titles verbose. Names exceeding 60 characters flagged warning: Rename manually something concise:
code: df <- decode_names(df, max_nchar = 60) #> ! 1 column name longer than 60 characters - consider renaming manually: #> • date_of_attending_assessment_centre_i0 names(df)[names(df) == "date_of_attending_assessment_centre_i0"] <- "date_baseline"

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
text: ?decode_values, ?decode_names vignette("extract") — extracting phenotype data GitHub Issues

title: Survival Analysis Setup for UKB Outcomes
text: disease case definitions derived (see vignette("derive")), three additional functions prepare data time--event analysis: Prerequisite: {name}_status, {name}_date, date_baseline, age_at_recruitment must already present — produced vignette("derive").

what: Step 1: Classify Timing — Prevalent vs. Incident
title: Survival Analysis Setup for UKB Outcomes
text: derive_timing() compares disease date UKB baseline assessment date assigns participant one four categories: Supply explicit column names defaults apply: Call per variable needed — example, combined case per individual source (HES, self-report, etc.).
code: library(ukbflow)  # Uses {name}_status and {name}_date by default df <- derive_timing(df, name = "outcome", baseline_col = "date_baseline") #> ✔ derive_timing (outcome_timing): #>   0 (no disease): 484 201 #>   1 (prevalent):   11 342 #>   2 (incident):     6 554 #>   NA (no date):         0 df <- derive_timing(df,   name         = "outcome_icd10",   status_col   = "outcome_icd10",   date_col     = "outcome_icd10_date",   baseline_col = "date_baseline" )

title: Survival Analysis Setup for UKB Outcomes
text: derive_age() computes age time event cases, returns NA non-cases cases without date. age_at_event=age_at_recruitment+event_date−baseline_date365.25\text{age\_at\_event} = \text{age\_at\_recruitment} + \frac{\text{event\_date} - \text{baseline\_date}}{365.25} divisor 365.25 accounts leap years, ensuring sub-monthly precision age calculation across full UKB follow-window. Supply explicit column mappings names follow default {name}_date / {name}_status pattern:
code: # Multiple outcomes in one call — auto-detects {name}_date and {name}_status df <- derive_age(df,   name         = c("exposure", "outcome"),   baseline_col = "date_baseline",   age_col      = "age_at_recruitment" ) #>   age_at_exposure: n=15 902, median=54.1, range=[40.2, 72.8] #>   age_at_outcome: n=8 104, median=61.7, range=[40.8, 78.3] #> ✔ derive_age: 2 events processed. df <- derive_age(df,   name         = "outcome_icd10",   baseline_col = "date_baseline",   age_col      = "age_at_recruitment",   date_cols    = c(outcome_icd10 = "outcome_icd10_date"),   status_cols  = c(outcome_icd10 = "outcome_icd10") )

title: Survival Analysis Setup for UKB Outcomes
text: derive_followup() computes follow-end date earliest : outcome event date (participant case) Date death (field 40000; competing event) Date lost follow-(field 191) administrative censoring date Follow-time years derived baseline date. Output columns:
code: df <- derive_followup(df,   name         = "outcome",   event_col    = "outcome_date",   baseline_col = "date_baseline",   censor_date  = as.Date("2022-06-01"),   # set to your study's cut-off date   death_col    = "date_death",   lost_col     = "date_lost_followup" ) #> ✔ derive_followup (outcome): #>   outcome_followup_end: 502 097 / 502 097 non-missing #>   outcome_followup_years: mean=11.84, median=12.31, range=[0.00, 14.92]

what: Auto-detection of death and lost-to-follow-up columns
title: Survival Analysis Setup for UKB Outcomes
text: death_col lost_col NULL (default), derive_followup() looks automatically field cache (UKB fields 40000 191). Pass FALSE explicitly disable competing event:
code: df <- derive_followup(df,   name         = "outcome",   event_col    = "outcome_date",   baseline_col = "date_baseline",   censor_date  = as.Date("2022-06-01"),   death_col    = FALSE,   lost_col     = FALSE )

title: Survival Analysis Setup for UKB Outcomes
text: completing three steps, data contains everything needed fit Cox proportional hazards model: Column roles model:
code: library(survival)  # Incident analysis: exclude participants with prevalent disease at baseline df_incident <- df[outcome_timing != 1L | is.na(outcome_timing)]  fit <- coxph(   Surv(outcome_followup_years, outcome_status) ~     exposure_status + age_at_recruitment + sex + smoking_status_i0,   data = df_incident ) summary(fit)

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_* functions convert raw, decoded UKB columns analysis-ready variables. vignette covers disease phenotype derivation pipeline: functions accept data.frame data.table return data.table new columns added -place (copy data.table input). Prerequisite: data extracted extract_pheno() extract_batch(), decoded decode_values() decode_names(). See vignette("decode").

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: field IDs universal across UKB projects. Include fields relevant analysis:
code: library(ukbflow)  df <- extract_pheno(c(   31,      # sex   21022,   # age at recruitment   53,      # date of baseline assessment (all visit instances)   20116,   # smoking status   # --- self-reported non-cancer illness ---   20002,   # illness codes   20008,   # year of diagnosis   # --- self-reported cancer ---   20001,   # cancer codes   20006,   # year of cancer diagnosis   # --- HES inpatient ---   41270,   # ICD-10 code array   41280,   # diagnosis date array   # --- cancer registry ---   40006,   # ICD-10 type   40005,   # date of cancer diagnosis   40011,   # histology   40012,   # behaviour   # --- death registry ---   40001,   # primary cause of death   40002,   # secondary cause of death   40000,   # date of death   # --- censoring / competing events ---   191      # date lost to follow-up )) |>   decode_values() |>   decode_names()  # Rename the verbose baseline date column to something concise names(df)[names(df) == "date_of_attending_assessment_centre_i0"] <- "date_baseline"

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: UKB uses special labels "know" "Prefer answer" distinguish refusal true missing data. derive_missing() converts NA (default) retains "Unknown" modelling. Performance: derive_missing() uses data.table::set() -place replacement — column copies made regardless dataset size. Processing 500 000 rows typically completes second negligible memory overhead. keep non-response model category: add custom labels beyond built-list:
code: df <- derive_missing(df) #> ✔ derive_missing: replaced 4 219 values across 5 columns (action = "na"). df <- derive_missing(df, action = "unknown") df <- derive_missing(df, extra_labels = "Not applicable")

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_covariate() converts character-encoded numeric columns numeric categorical columns factor, printing distribution summary .
code: df <- derive_covariate(df,   as_numeric = "body_mass_index_bmi_i0",   as_factor  = c("sex", "smoking_status_i0"),   factor_levels = list(     smoking_status_i0 = c("Never", "Previous", "Current")   ) ) #> ── Numeric ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>   body_mass_index_bmi_i0: mean=27.4, median=26.8, sd=4.9, Q1=24.1, Q3=30.1, NA=0.9% (n=4512) #> ── Factor ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>   sex [2 levels] #>     Female: n=273 029 (54.4%) #>     Male:   n=229 068 (45.6%) #>   smoking_status_i0 [3 levels] #>     Never:    n=271 400 (54.1%) #>     Previous: n=150 736 (30.0%) #>     Current:  n=79 867 (15.9%)

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_cut() creates new factor column binning continuous variable quantile-based custom groups.
code: # Equal-frequency tertiles (automatic breakpoints) df <- derive_cut(df, col = "age_at_recruitment", n = 3) # → adds age_at_recruitment_tri with levels Q1 / Q2 / Q3 # Custom breakpoints with meaningful labels df <- derive_cut(df,   col    = "age_at_recruitment",   n      = 3,   breaks = c(50, 60),   labels = c("<50", "50–59", "≥60"),   name   = "age_group" )

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_selfreport() searches UKB self-reported non-cancer illness (field 20002) cancer (field 20001) columns disease label matching regex, returns binary status earliest report date. adds two columns:
code: # Non-cancer illness example df <- derive_selfreport(df,   name  = "disease",   regex = "your disease name|synonym" ) #> ✔ derive_selfreport (disease): 8104 cases, 8104 with date.  # Cancer illness example (field = "cancer") df <- derive_selfreport(df,   name  = "cancer_outcome",   regex = "your cancer name",   field = "cancer" )

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_hes() scans UKB Hospital Episode Statistics ICD-10 codes (field 41270, stored JSON array per participant) matches earliest corresponding date field 41280. match argument controls codes compared: match = "regex" accepts full Perl-compatible regular expressions, making straightforward capture related ICD-10 codes across chapters subcategories single call (e.g. "^E1[01]" captures E10 E11 simultaneously).
code: # Prefix match: codes starting with "E11" (e.g. E110, E119) df <- derive_hes(df, name = "disease", icd10 = "E11")  # Exact match: only "E11" itself df <- derive_hes(df, name = "disease", icd10 = "E11", match = "exact")  # Regex: full control over the pattern df <- derive_hes(df, name = "disease", icd10 = "^E1[01]", match = "regex")

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: UKB First Occurrence fields (p131xxx) record earliest date condition observed across linked sources — self-report, HES inpatient, GP records, death registry — pre-integrated UKB. disease corresponding 131xxx field, usually fastest comprehensive single-source case definition. Look disease UKB Field Finder.
code: # Look up your disease's First Occurrence field ID in the UKB Field Finder, # then replace fo_field_id accordingly. fo_field_id <- 131000L   # <-- replace with your field ID  df <- derive_first_occurrence(df, name = "disease", field = fo_field_id) #> ✔ derive_first_occurrence (disease): 12043 cases with valid date.

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_cancer_registry() searches cancer registry ICD-10 field (40006) optionally filters histology (field 40011) behaviour (field 40012).
code: # ICD-10 only — no histology/behaviour filter df <- derive_cancer_registry(df,   name  = "cancer_outcome",   icd10 = "^C50"   # breast cancer example ) #> ✔ derive_cancer_registry (cancer_outcome): 2 318 cases.  # With histology and behaviour filters df <- derive_cancer_registry(df,   name      = "cancer_outcome",   icd10     = "^C44",   histology = c(8070, 8071, 8072),   # specify relevant morphology codes   behaviour = 3L                      # 3 = malignant )

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_death_registry() searches primary (field 40001) secondary (field 40002) causes death ICD-10 codes.
code: df <- derive_death_registry(df, name = "disease", icd10 = "E11") #> ✔ derive_death_registry (disease): 312 cases, 312 with date.

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_icd10() high-level wrapper calls combination source-specific functions merges outputs single status column earliest date. Intermediate source columns retained alongside combined result:
code: # Non-cancer disease: HES + death + First Occurrence df <- derive_icd10(df,   name     = "disease",   icd10    = "E11",   source   = c("hes", "death", "first_occurrence"),   fo_field = fo_field_id   # defined above ) #> ✔ derive_icd10 (disease): 14 287 cases across 3 sources, 14 287 with date.  # Cancer outcome: HES + cancer registry + death df <- derive_icd10(df,   name   = "cancer_outcome",   icd10  = "^C50",   match  = "regex",   source = c("hes", "death", "cancer_registry") )

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_case() merges self-report ICD-10 flags unified case status, earliest date across sources taken via pmin(). Output columns: earliest date matters: disease_date captures true first occurrence disease regardless source recorded first. column direct input derive_timing(), derive_age(), derive_followup() — chronological anchor every downstream survival analysis.
code: df <- derive_case(df, name = "disease") #> ✔ derive_case (disease): 15 902 cases, 15 902 with date. #>   Both sources (disease_icd10 & disease_selfreport): 6 218

Rather switching terminal using dx commands directly, can browse remote project structure retrieve files entirely within R session. code: # List project root fetch_ls() #>                        name   type      size            modified #> 1           Showcase metadata/ folder      <NA>                <NA> #> 2                    results/ folder      <NA>                <NA> #> 3              analysis.log   file    4.2 KB 2024-11-01 10:22:03  # List a specific folder fetch_ls("Showcase metadata/") #>          name  type     size            modified #> 1   field.tsv  file  12.3 MB 2024-10-15 08:01:44 #> 2 esimpint.tsv  file   3.1 MB 2024-10-15 08:01:50  # Filter by type fetch_ls("results/", type = "file")  # Filter by name pattern fetch_ls("results/", pattern = "\\.csv$")

title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
text: grs_* functions provide end--end pipeline computing validating polygenic / genetic risk scores within UK Biobank Research Analysis Platform (RAP). individual-level genotype data downloaded locally, computationally intensive steps executed cloud jobs via DNAnexus Swiss Army Knife app. Typical pipeline: Prerequisite: must authenticated UKB RAP project selected. See vignette("auth").
code: grs_check()  ->  grs_bgen2pgen()  ->  grs_score()  ->  grs_standardize()  ->  grs_validate()

what: Step 1: Validate the Weights File – grs_check()
title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
text: uploading RAP, validate SNP weights file. function reads file, checks required columns, flags problems, writes plink2-compatible space-delimited output. Required columns: function returns validated data.table invisibly, can passed directly downstream functions.
code: library(ukbflow)  # Local: weights file on your machine w <- grs_check("weights.csv", dest = "weights_clean.txt") #> Read 'weights.csv': 312 rows, 5 columns. #> ✔ No NA values. #> ✔ No duplicate SNPs. #> ✔ All SNP IDs match rs[0-9]+ format. #> ✔ All effect alleles are A/T/C/G. #> Beta summary: #>   Range     : -0.412 to 0.589 #>   Mean |beta|: 0.183 #>   Positive  : 187 (59.9%) #>   Negative  : 125 (40.1%) #>   Zero      : 0 #> ✔ Weights file passed checks: 312 SNPs ready for UKB RAP. #> ✔ Saved: 'weights_clean.txt' # On RAP (JupyterLab / RStudio) -- use /mnt/project/ paths directly w <- grs_check(   file = "/mnt/project/weights/weights.csv",   dest = "/mnt/project/weights/weights_clean.txt" )

what: Step 2: Convert BGEN to PGEN – grs_bgen2pgen()
title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
text: UKB imputed genotype data stored BGEN format RAP. grs_bgen2pgen() submits one Swiss Army Knife job per chromosome convert BGEN files faster PGEN format MAF filter applied via plink2. function called local machine RAP JupyterLab – heavy lifting runs entirely cloud. Instance types: Key arguments: Storage warning: chromosomes 1–14 may exceed 200 GB SSD "standard" instances. Use instance = "large" .
code: # Test on the smallest chromosome first ids <- grs_bgen2pgen(chr = 22, priority = "high") #> Uploading 'grs_bgen2pgen_std.R' to RAP ... #> ✔ Uploaded: '/grs_bgen2pgen_std.R' #> Submitting 1 job(s) -- mem2_ssd1_v2_x4 / priority: high #> ✔ chr22 -> 'job-Gxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx' #> ✔ 1/1 job(s) submitted. Monitor with job_ls(). # Full run: small chromosomes on standard, large on upgraded instance ids_small <- grs_bgen2pgen(chr = 15:22) ids_large <- grs_bgen2pgen(chr = 1:14, instance = "large")  # Monitor progress job_ls() job_wait(c(ids_small, ids_large))

title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
text: PGEN files ready, grs_score() uploads weights file(s) submits one plink2 scoring job per GRS. job scores 22 chromosomes saves single CSV RAP. running RAP JupyterLab weights already project root, upload step skipped automatically: Important: maf argument must match value used grs_bgen2pgen() correct PGEN files located. Output per job: /grs/<GRS_name>_scores.csv columns IID GRS score named GRS_<name>.
code: ids <- grs_score(   file = c(     grs_a = "weights/grs_a_weights.txt",     grs_b = "weights/grs_b_weights.txt"   ),   dest     = "/grs/",   priority = "high" ) #> ── Uploading 2 weight file(s) to RAP ──────────────────────────────────────── #> Uploading 'grs_a_weights.txt' ... #> ✔ Uploaded: '/grs_a_weights.txt' #> Uploading 'grs_b_weights.txt' ... #> ✔ Uploaded: '/grs_b_weights.txt' #> Submitting 2 job(s) -- mem2_ssd1_v2_x4 / priority: high #> ✔ grs_a -> 'job-Gxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx' #> ✔ grs_b -> 'job-Gyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyy' #> ✔ 2/2 job(s) submitted. Monitor with job_ls().  job_wait(ids) # On RAP: weights already at /mnt/project/grs_a_weights.txt ids <- grs_score(   file = c(grs_a = "/mnt/project/grs_a_weights.txt"),   dest = "/grs/" ) #> ℹ grs_a_weights.txt already at RAP root, skipping upload.

title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
text: downloading score CSVs RAP (via fetch_file()) merging analysis dataset, Z-score standardise GRS column effect estimates interpretable per-SD associations. grs_zscore() alias produces identical result. original columns preserved; _z columns inserted immediately source column.
code: # Auto-detect all columns containing "grs" (case-insensitive) cohort <- grs_standardize(cohort) #> Auto-detected 2 GRS column(s): 'GRS_a', 'GRS_b' #> ✔ GRS_a -> GRS_a_z  [mean=0.0000, sd=1.0000] #> ✔ GRS_b -> GRS_b_z  [mean=0.0000, sd=1.0000] # Or specify columns explicitly cohort <- grs_standardize(cohort, grs_cols = c("GRS_a", "GRS_b"))

title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
text: grs_validate() runs four complementary validation analyses GRS:

title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
text: Return value: named list four data.table elements. AUC calculation requires pROC package. Install install.packages("pROC") needed.
code: res <- grs_validate(   data        = cohort,   grs_cols    = c("GRS_a_z", "GRS_b_z"),   outcome_col = "outcome",   time_col    = "followup_years",   covariates  = c("age", "sex", paste0("pc", 1:10)) )  res$per_sd          # HR per SD x model res$high_vs_low     # HR: top 20% vs bottom 20% res$trend           # p-trend across Q1–Q4 res$discrimination  # C-index with 95% CI

title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
code: library(ukbflow)  # 1. Validate weights (local) grs_check("weights.csv", dest = "weights_clean.txt")  # 2. Convert BGEN -> PGEN on RAP (submit jobs) ids_std  <- grs_bgen2pgen(chr = 15:22, maf = 0.01) ids_lrg  <- grs_bgen2pgen(chr = 1:14,  maf = 0.01, instance = "large") job_wait(c(ids_std, ids_lrg))  # 3. Score GRS on RAP (submit jobs) score_ids <- grs_score(   file = c(grs_a = "weights_clean.txt"),   maf  = 0.01,   # must match grs_bgen2pgen()   dest = "/grs/" ) job_wait(score_ids)  # 4. Download score CSV from RAP fetch_file("/grs/GRS_a_scores.csv", dest = "GRS_a_scores.csv")  # 5. Merge into cohort and standardise scores  <- data.table::fread("GRS_a_scores.csv") cohort  <- scores[cohort, on = "IID"] cohort  <- grs_standardize(cohort, grs_cols = "GRS_a")  # 6. Validate res <- grs_validate(   data        = cohort,   grs_cols    = "GRS_a_z",   outcome_col = "outcome",   time_col    = "followup_years",   covariates  = c("age", "sex", paste0("pc", 1:10)) )  res$per_sd res$discrimination

title: Publication-Ready Visualisation
text: Two functions produce publication-ready figures tables minimal post-processing: functions save formats single call return plot/table object invisibly customisation.

title: Publication-Ready Visualisation
text: plot_forest() takes data frame whose first column row label, plus additional display columns. CI graphic formatted (95% CI) text column inserted automatically.
code: library(ukbflow)  df <- data.frame(   item      = c("Exposure vs. control", "Unadjusted", "Fully adjusted"),   `Cases/N` = c("", "89 / 4 521", "89 / 4 521"),   p_value   = c(NA_real_, 0.001, 0.006),   check.names = FALSE )  p <- plot_forest(   data      = df,   est       = c(NA,   1.52, 1.43),   lower     = c(NA,   1.18, 1.11),   upper     = c(NA,   1.96, 1.85),   ci_column = 2L,   indent    = c(0L,   1L,   1L),   p_cols    = "p_value",   xlim      = c(0.5,  3.0) ) plot(p)

what: Building the input data frame from assoc_*() results
title: Publication-Ready Visualisation
text: output assoc_coxph() (siblings) can reshaped directly format expected plot_forest():
code: res <- assoc_coxph(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   time_col     = "outcome_followup_years",   exposure_col = "exposure",   covariates   = c("age_at_recruitment", "sex", "tdi", "smoking_status_i0") )  # Reshape: one row per model, label column first df <- data.frame(   item    = c("Exposure", as.character(res$model)),   `N`     = c("", paste0(res$n, " / ", res$n_events)),   p_value = c(NA_real_, res$p_value),   check.names = FALSE )  p <- plot_forest(   data      = df,   est       = c(NA,   res$HR),   lower     = c(NA,   res$CI_lower),   upper     = c(NA,   res$CI_upper),   ci_column = 2L,   indent    = c(0L,   rep(1L, nrow(res))),   p_cols    = "p_value",   xlim      = c(0.5,  2.5),   save      = TRUE,   dest      = "forest_exposure"   # saves .png / .pdf / .jpg / .tiff )

title: Publication-Ready Visualisation
text: CI appearance Row labels indentation P-value formatting Background borders Layout saving four formats (PNG, PDF, JPG, TIFF) written 300 dpi white background. function returns plot object invisibly; display plot(p) grid::grid.draw(p).
code: p <- plot_forest(   data      = df,   est       = est, lower = lower, upper = upper,   ci_column = 2L,   ci_col    = c("grey50", "steelblue", "steelblue"),  # per-row colours   ci_sizes  = 0.5,       # point size   ci_Theight = 0.15,     # cap height   ref_line  = 1,         # reference line (use 0 for beta coefficients)   xlim      = c(0.2, 5), ticks_at = c(0.5, 1, 2, 3) ) # indent = 0 → bold parent row; indent >= 1 → indented sub-row (plain) p <- plot_forest(   data       = df,   est        = est, lower = lower, upper = upper,   ci_column  = 2L,   indent     = c(0L, 1L, 1L),        # parent + 2 sub-rows   bold_label = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE)  # explicit control (overrides indent default) ) # p_cols: column names in data that contain raw numeric p-values. # Values < 10^(-p_digits) are displayed as e.g. "<0.001". # bold_p = TRUE bolds all p < p_threshold (default 0.05). p <- plot_forest(   data        = df,   est         = est, lower = lower, upper = upper,   ci_column   = 2L,   p_cols      = "p_value",   p_digits    = 3L,   bold_p      = TRUE,   p_threshold = 0.05 ) p <- plot_forest(   data       = df,   est        = est, lower = lower, upper = upper,   ci_column  = 2L,   background = "zebra",       # "zebra" | "bold_label" | "none"   bg_col     = "#F0F0F0",     # shading colour   border     = "three_line",  # "three_line" | "none"   border_width = 3            # scalar or length-3 vector (top / mid / bottom) ) p <- plot_forest(   data        = df,   est         = est, lower = lower, upper = upper,   ci_column   = 2L,   row_height  = NULL,   # auto (8 / 12 / 10 / 15 mm); or scalar/vector   col_width   = NULL,   # auto (rounds up to nearest 5 mm)   save        = TRUE,   dest        = "results/forest_main",   # extension ignored; all 4 formats saved   save_width  = 20,     # cm   save_height = NULL    # auto: nrow(data) * 0.9 + 3 cm )

title: ukbflow
text: ukbflow provides streamlined, RAP-native R workflow UK Biobank analysis — phenotype extraction disease derivation association analysis publication-quality figures. functions designed run within UK Biobank Research Analysis Platform (RAP), compliance 2024+ data policy requiring individual-level data remain cloud.

title: ukbflow
text: Requirements: R ≥ 4.1 · dxpy (dx-toolkit, required RAP interaction)
code: # Recommended pak::pkg_install("evanbio/ukbflow")  # or remotes::install_github("evanbio/ukbflow") pip install dxpy

title: ukbflow
text: Connection — Authenticate RAP via dx-toolkit manage project selection (auth_login, auth_select_project) Data Access — Retrieve phenotype data UKB dataset RAP; monitor asynchronous jobs (fetch_metadata, extract_batch, job_wait) Data Processing — Harmonize multi-source records derive analysis-ready cohort: decode field IDs value codes, build ICD-10 case definitions, compute follow-time (decode_names, decode_values, derive_icd10, derive_followup, derive_case) Association Analysis — Cox, logistic, linear regression automatic three-model adjustment framework; subgroup analysis, dose-response trend, Fine-Gray competing risks (assoc_coxph, assoc_logistic, assoc_subgroup) Genomic Scoring — Distributed plink2 scoring RAP worker nodes: BGEN → PGEN conversion, multi-chromosome GRS computation, standardisation (grs_bgen2pgen, grs_score, grs_standardize) Visualization — Publication-ready forest plots Table 1, saved major formats 300 dpi (plot_forest, plot_tableone)

title: ukbflow
code: library(ukbflow)  # Authenticate auth_login() auth_select_project("project-XXXXXXXXXXXX")  # Extract and decode df <- extract_pheno(c(31, 21022, 53, 20116, 41270, 41280)) |>   decode_values() |>   decode_names()  # Derive disease phenotype + follow-up df <- df |>   derive_missing() |>   derive_icd10(name = "outcome", icd10 = "E11",                source = c("hes", "first_occurrence")) |>   derive_followup(name = "outcome", event_col = "outcome_date",                   baseline_col = "date_baseline",                   censor_date  = as.Date("2022-06-01"))  # Association analysis res <- assoc_coxph(df,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   time_col     = "outcome_followup_years",   exposure_col = "exposure_status",   covariates   = c("age_at_recruitment", "sex", "tdi") )  # Forest plot plot_forest(res)

what: Fine-Gray competing risks association analysis — assoc_competing
title: Fine-Gray competing risks association analysis — assoc_competing
text: Fits Fine-Gray subdistribution hazard model (via survival::finegray() + weighted coxph()) exposure variable returns tidy result table subdistribution hazard ratios (SHR).

title: Fine-Gray competing risks association analysis — assoc_competing
code: assoc_competing(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col,   exposure_col,   compete_col = NULL,   event_val = 1L,   compete_val = 2L,   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   conf_level = 0.95 )  assoc_fg(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col,   exposure_col,   compete_col = NULL,   event_val = 1L,   compete_val = 2L,   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   conf_level = 0.95 )

title: Fine-Gray competing risks association analysis — assoc_competing
text: data (data.frame data.table) Analysis dataset. outcome_col (character) Primary event column. Mode : multi-value column (integer character codes). Mode B: 0/1 binary column. time_col (character) Follow-time column (numeric, e.g. years). exposure_col (character) One exposure variable names. compete_col (character NULL) Mode B : name 0/1 competing event column. NULL (default), Mode used. event_val Scalar value outcome_col indicating primary event (Mode ). Default: 1L. compete_val Scalar value outcome_col indicating competing event (Mode ). Default: 2L. covariates (character NULL) Covariate names Fully adjusted model. NULL, Unadjusted (Age/sex adjusted base = TRUE) run. base (logical) Whether auto-detect age sex columns include Age sex adjusted model. Default: TRUE. conf_level (numeric) Confidence level SHR intervals. Default: 0.95.

title: Fine-Gray competing risks association analysis — assoc_competing
text: data.table one row per exposure \(\times\) term \(\times\) model combination: exposure Exposure variable name. term Coefficient name returned coxph. model Ordered factor: Unadjusted < Age sex adjusted < Fully adjusted. n Participants model (NA removal). n_events Primary events analysis set. n_compete Competing events analysis set. SHR Subdistribution hazard ratio. CI_lower Lower CI bound. CI_upper Upper CI bound. p_value Robust z-test p-value weighted Cox. SHR_label Formatted string, e.g. "1.23 (1.05--1.44)".

title: Fine-Gray competing risks association analysis — assoc_competing
text: Two input modes supported depending outcome coded dataset: Mode - single multi-value column compete_col = NULL (default). outcome_col contains event codes one column (e.g. 0/1/2/3). Use event_val compete_val identify event interest competing event; values treated censored. Example: UKB censoring_type 1 = event, 2 = death (competing), 0/3 = censored. Mode B - dual binary columns compete_col name separate 0/1 column competing event. outcome_col 0/1 column primary event. 1 participant, primary event takes priority. Example: outcome_col = "cscc_status", compete_col = "death_status". Internally modes converted three-level factor c("censor", "event", "compete") passed finegray(). Three adjustment models produced (data allow): Unadjusted - always included. Age sex adjusted - base = TRUE age/sex columns detected. Fully adjusted - covariates non-NULL.

title: Fine-Gray competing risks association analysis — assoc_competing
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Mode A: single multi-value column (0 = censored, 1 = event, 2 = competing) assoc_competing(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "censoring_type",   time_col     = "followup_years",   exposure_col = "exposure",   event_val    = 1L,   compete_val  = 2L,   covariates   = c("tdi", "smoking") )  # Mode B: separate 0/1 columns for primary and competing events assoc_competing(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   time_col     = "followup_years",   exposure_col = c("exposure", "bmi_category"),   compete_col  = "death_status",   covariates   = c("tdi", "smoking") ) } # }

what: Cox proportional hazards association analysis — assoc_coxph
title: Cox proportional hazards association analysis — assoc_coxph
text: Fits one Cox models exposure variable returns tidy result table suitable downstream forest plots. default, two standard adjustment models always included alongside user-specified model:

title: Cox proportional hazards association analysis — assoc_coxph
code: assoc_coxph(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col,   exposure_col,   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   strata = NULL,   conf_level = 0.95 )  assoc_cox(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col,   exposure_col,   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   strata = NULL,   conf_level = 0.95 )

title: Cox proportional hazards association analysis — assoc_coxph
text: data (data.frame data.table) Analysis dataset. Must contain columns referenced outcome_col, time_col, exposure_col. outcome_col (character) Name event indicator column. Accepts logical (TRUE/FALSE) numeric/integer (0/1). time_col (character) Name follow-time column (numeric, consistent units, e.g. years). exposure_col (character) One exposure variable names. variable analysed separately; results stacked row-wise. covariates (character NULL) Additional covariate column names Fully adjusted model (e.g. c("tdi", "smoking", paste0("pc", 1:10))). NULL (default), Fully adjusted model run. base (logical) TRUE (default), always include Unadjusted Age sex adjusted models addition user-specified covariates model. Set FALSE run Fully adjusted model (requires covariates non-NULL). strata (character NULL) Optional stratification variable. Passed survival::strata() Cox formula. conf_level (numeric) Confidence level hazard ratio intervals. Default: 0.95.

title: Cox proportional hazards association analysis — assoc_coxph
text: data.table one row per exposure \(\times\) term \(\times\) model combination, following columns: exposure Exposure variable name. term Coefficient name returned coxph (e.g. "bmi_categoryObese" factor, variable name numeric/binary exposures). model Ordered factor: Unadjusted < Age sex adjusted < Fully adjusted. n Number participants included model (NA removal). n_events Total number events dataset. person_years Total person-years follow-(rounded). HR Hazard ratio (point estimate). CI_lower Lower bound confidence interval. CI_upper Upper bound confidence interval. p_value Wald test p-value. HR_label Formatted string, e.g. "1.23 (1.05-1.44)".

title: Cox proportional hazards association analysis — assoc_coxph
text: Unadjusted - covariates (crude). Age sex adjusted - age + sex auto-detected data via UKB field IDs (21022 31). Skipped warning either column found. Fully adjusted - covariates supplied via covariates argument. run covariates non-NULL. Outcome coding: outcome_col may logical (TRUE/FALSE) integer/numeric (0/1). Logical values converted integer internally. Exposure types supported: Binary - 0/1 TRUE/FALSE; produces one term row per model. Factor - produces one term row per non-reference level. Numeric (continuous) - produces one term row per model.

title: Cox proportional hazards association analysis — assoc_coxph
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Minimal: crude + age-sex adjusted only res <- assoc_coxph(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   # 0/1 or TRUE/FALSE   time_col     = "followup_years",   exposure_col = c("exposure", "bmi_category") )  # Add a Fully adjusted model (Model 3) res <- assoc_coxph(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   time_col     = "followup_years",   exposure_col = "exposure",   covariates   = c("tdi", "smoking", "alcohol_freq",                    paste0("pc", 1:10)) )  # Only run the Fully adjusted model (skip Unadjusted + Age-sex) res <- assoc_coxph(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   time_col     = "followup_years",   exposure_col = "exposure",   covariates   = c("age_at_recruitment", "sex", "tdi"),   base         = FALSE ) } # }

what: Proportional hazards assumption test for Cox regression — assoc_coxph_zph
title: Proportional hazards assumption test for Cox regression — assoc_coxph_zph
text: Tests proportional hazards (PH) assumption using Schoenfeld residuals via cox.zph. Re-fits models assoc_coxph (interface) returns tidy result table term-level global test statistics.

title: Proportional hazards assumption test for Cox regression — assoc_coxph_zph
code: assoc_coxph_zph(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col,   exposure_col,   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   strata = NULL )  assoc_zph(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col,   exposure_col,   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   strata = NULL )

title: Proportional hazards assumption test for Cox regression — assoc_coxph_zph
text: data (data.frame data.table) Analysis dataset. outcome_col (character) Binary event indicator (0/1 TRUE/FALSE). time_col (character) Follow-time column name. exposure_col (character) One exposure variable names. covariates (character NULL) Covariates Fully adjusted model. Default: NULL. base (logical) Include Unadjusted Age sex adjusted models. Default: TRUE. strata (character NULL) Optional stratification variable.

title: Proportional hazards assumption test for Cox regression — assoc_coxph_zph
text: data.table one row per exposure \(\times\) term \(\times\) model combination, columns: exposure Exposure variable name. term Coefficient name. model Ordered factor: Unadjusted < Age sex adjusted < Fully adjusted. chisq Schoenfeld residual chi-squared statistic. df Degrees freedom. p_value P-value PH test (term-level). ph_satisfied Logical; TRUE p_value > 0.05. global_chisq Global chi-squared whole model. global_df Global degrees freedom. global_p Global p-value whole model.

title: Proportional hazards assumption test for Cox regression — assoc_coxph_zph
text: non-significant p-value (p > 0.05) indicates PH assumption satisfied term. global test (global_p) reflects overall PH assumption whole model.

title: Proportional hazards assumption test for Cox regression — assoc_coxph_zph
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Check PH assumption for same models as assoc_coxph() zph <- assoc_coxph_zph(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   time_col     = "followup_years",   exposure_col = c("exposure", "bmi_category"),   covariates   = c("tdi", "smoking", paste0("pc", 1:10)) )  # Quick check: any violations? zph[ph_satisfied == FALSE] } # }

what: Cox regression lag sensitivity analysis — assoc_lag
title: Cox regression lag sensitivity analysis — assoc_lag
text: Runs Cox proportional hazards models one lag periods assess whether associations robust exclusion early events. lag, participants whose follow-time less lag_years removed analysis dataset; follow-time kept original scale (shifted). mirrors approach used UK Biobank sensitivity analyses address reverse causation detection bias.

title: Cox regression lag sensitivity analysis — assoc_lag
code: assoc_lag(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col,   exposure_col,   lag_years = c(1, 2),   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   strata = NULL,   conf_level = 0.95 )

title: Cox regression lag sensitivity analysis — assoc_lag
text: data (data.frame data.table) Analysis dataset. outcome_col (character) Event indicator column (0/1 logical). time_col (character) Follow-time column (numeric, e.g. years). exposure_col (character) One exposure variable names. lag_years (numeric) One lag periods units time_col. Default: c(1, 2). Use 0 include unfiltered full-cohort result reference. covariates (character NULL) Covariates Fully adjusted model. NULL, Unadjusted (Age sex adjusted base = TRUE) run. base (logical) Auto-detect age sex include Age sex adjusted model. Default: TRUE. strata (character NULL) Optional stratification variable passed survival::strata(). conf_level (numeric) Confidence level HR intervals. Default: 0.95.

title: Cox regression lag sensitivity analysis — assoc_lag
text: data.table one row per lag \(\times\) exposure \(\times\) term \(\times\) model combination, containing columns produced assoc_coxph plus: lag_years lag period applied (numeric). n_excluded Number participants excluded follow-time less lag_years. lag_years n_excluded placed immediately model column order.

title: Cox regression lag sensitivity analysis — assoc_lag
text: Setting lag_years = 0 (including 0 vector) runs model full unfiltered cohort, providing reference lagged results can compared. three adjustment models produced assoc_coxph available (Unadjusted, Age sex adjusted, Fully adjusted).

title: Cox regression lag sensitivity analysis — assoc_lag
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ assoc_lag(   data         = ukb_df,   outcome_col  = "cscc_status",   time_col     = "followup_years",   exposure_col = "ad_tf",   lag_years    = c(0, 1, 2),   covariates   = c("tdi", "smoking") ) } # }

title: Linear regression association analysis — assoc_linear
code: assoc_linear(   data,   outcome_col,   exposure_col,   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   conf_level = 0.95 )  assoc_lm(   data,   outcome_col,   exposure_col,   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   conf_level = 0.95 )

title: Linear regression association analysis — assoc_linear
text: data (data.frame data.table) Analysis dataset. outcome_col (character) Name continuous numeric outcome column. exposure_col (character) One exposure variable names. covariates (character NULL) Covariate column names Fully adjusted model. Default: NULL. base (logical) Include Unadjusted Age sex adjusted models. Default: TRUE. conf_level (numeric) Confidence level. Default: 0.95.

title: Linear regression association analysis — assoc_linear
text: data.table one row per exposure \(\times\) term \(\times\) model combination, columns: exposure Exposure variable name. term Coefficient name (e.g. "bmi_categoryObese"). model Ordered factor: Unadjusted < Age sex adjusted < Fully adjusted. n Participants model (NA removal). beta Regression coefficient (\(\beta\)). se Standard error \(\beta\). CI_lower Lower confidence bound. CI_upper Upper confidence bound. p_value t-test p-value. beta_label Formatted string, e.g. "0.23 (0.05-0.41)".

title: Linear regression association analysis — assoc_linear
text: Unadjusted - covariates (crude). Age sex adjusted - age + sex auto-detected data via UKB field IDs (21022 31). Skipped warning either column found. Fully adjusted - covariates supplied via covariates argument. run covariates non-NULL. Outcome: must continuous numeric variable. Passing binary (0/1) logical column trigger warning, logistic regression appropriate case. CI method: based t-distribution via confint.lm(), exact normal linear model assumption. ci_method argument (unlike assoc_logistic) profile likelihood apply lm. SE column: standard error \(\beta\) included support downstream meta-analysis GWAS-style summary statistics.

title: Linear regression association analysis — assoc_linear
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Minimal: crude + age-sex adjusted res <- assoc_linear(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "bmi",   exposure_col = c("exposure", "smoking_pack_years") )  # With Fully adjusted model res <- assoc_linear(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "bmi",   exposure_col = "exposure",   covariates   = c("tdi", "alcohol_freq", paste0("pc", 1:10)) ) } # }

title: Logistic regression association analysis — assoc_logistic
text: data (data.frame data.table) Analysis dataset. outcome_col (character) Binary outcome column (0/1 TRUE/FALSE). exposure_col (character) One exposure variable names. covariates (character NULL) Covariate column names Fully adjusted model. Default: NULL. base (logical) Include Unadjusted Age sex adjusted models. Default: TRUE. ci_method (character) CI calculation method: "wald" (default) "profile". conf_level (numeric) Confidence level. Default: 0.95.

title: Logistic regression association analysis — assoc_logistic
text: data.table one row per exposure \(\times\) term \(\times\) model combination, columns: exposure Exposure variable name. term Coefficient name (e.g. "bmi_categoryObese"). model Ordered factor: Unadjusted < Age sex adjusted < Fully adjusted. n Participants model (NA removal). n_cases Number cases (outcome = 1) model. Odds ratio (point estimate). CI_lower Lower confidence bound. CI_upper Upper confidence bound. p_value Wald test p-value. OR_label Formatted string, e.g. "1.23 (1.05-1.44)".

title: Logistic regression association analysis — assoc_logistic
text: Unadjusted - covariates (crude). Age sex adjusted - age + sex auto-detected data via UKB field IDs (21022 31). Skipped warning either column found. Fully adjusted - covariates supplied via covariates argument. run covariates non-NULL. Outcome coding: outcome_col may logical (TRUE/FALSE) integer/numeric (0/1). Logical values converted integer internally. CI methods: "wald" (default) - fast, appropriate large UKB samples. "profile" - profile likelihood CI via confint.glm(); slower accurate small sparse data.

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Minimal: crude + age-sex adjusted res <- assoc_logistic(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   exposure_col = c("exposure", "bmi_category") )  # With Fully adjusted model + profile likelihood CI res <- assoc_logistic(   data         = cohort,   outcome_col  = "outcome_status",   exposure_col = "exposure",   covariates   = c("tdi", "smoking", paste0("pc", 1:10)),   ci_method    = "profile" ) } # }

what: Subgroup association analysis with optional interaction test — assoc_subgroup
title: Subgroup association analysis with optional interaction test — assoc_subgroup
text: Stratifies dataset single grouping variable () runs specified association model (coxph, logistic, linear) within subgroup. Unlike assoc_coxph siblings, automatic age--sex-adjusted model: subgroup level variable defines stratum zero variance, making auto-detected adjustment meaningless. Instead, two models available:

title: Subgroup association analysis with optional interaction test — assoc_subgroup
code: assoc_subgroup(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col = NULL,   exposure_col,   by,   method = c("coxph", "logistic", "linear"),   covariates = NULL,   interaction = TRUE,   conf_level = 0.95 )  assoc_sub(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col = NULL,   exposure_col,   by,   method = c("coxph", "logistic", "linear"),   covariates = NULL,   interaction = TRUE,   conf_level = 0.95 )

title: Subgroup association analysis with optional interaction test — assoc_subgroup
text: data (data.frame data.table) Analysis dataset. outcome_col (character) Outcome column name. time_col (character NULL) Follow-time column (required method = "coxph"). exposure_col (character) One exposure variable names. (character) Single stratification variable name. unique non-NA values (factor levels, order) define subgroups. method (character) Regression method: "coxph" (default), "logistic", "linear". covariates (character NULL) Covariate column names Fully adjusted model. NULL, Unadjusted model run. interaction (logical) Compute LRT p-value exposure \(\times\) interaction full dataset. Default: TRUE. conf_level (numeric) Confidence level. Default: 0.95.

title: Subgroup association analysis with optional interaction test — assoc_subgroup
text: data.table one row per subgroup level \(\times\) exposure \(\times\) term \(\times\) model, containing: subgroup Name variable. subgroup_level Factor: level variable (ordered original level / sort order). exposure Exposure variable name. term Coefficient name fitted model. model Ordered factor: Unadjusted < Fully adjusted. n Participants model (NA removal). ... Effect estimate columns: HR//beta, CI_lower, CI_upper, p_value, formatted label column. Cox models additionally include n_events person_years; logistic models include n_cases; linear models include se. p_interaction LRT p-value exposure \(\times\) interaction full dataset. Shared across subgroup levels exposure \(\times\) model. NA interaction model fails. present interaction = TRUE.

title: Subgroup association analysis with optional interaction test — assoc_subgroup
text: Unadjusted - always run (covariates). Fully adjusted - run covariates non-NULL. Users responsible excluding variable covariates (warning issued included). Interaction test: interaction = TRUE (default), likelihood ratio test (LRT) exposure \(\times\) interaction computed full dataset exposure \(\times\) model combination appended p_interaction. LRT preferred Wald handles factor, binary, continuous variables uniformly without requiring user recode variable.

code: assoc_trend(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col = NULL,   exposure_col,   method = c("coxph", "logistic", "linear"),   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   scores = NULL,   conf_level = 0.95 )  assoc_tr(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col = NULL,   exposure_col,   method = c("coxph", "logistic", "linear"),   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   scores = NULL,   conf_level = 0.95 )

title: Dose-response trend analysis — assoc_trend
text: data (data.frame data.table) Analysis dataset. outcome_col (character) Outcome column name. time_col (character NULL) Follow-time column (required method = "coxph"). exposure_col (character) One exposure column names. must factor (ordered unordered). first level used reference group. method (character) Regression method: "coxph" (default), "logistic", "linear". covariates (character NULL) Covariates Fully adjusted model. Default: NULL. base (logical) Include Unadjusted Age--sex-adjusted models. Default: TRUE. scores (numeric NULL) Numeric scores assigned factor levels level order. Length must equal nlevels every exposure. NULL (default) uses 0, 1, 2, ... conf_level (numeric) Confidence level. Default: 0.95.

title: Dose-response trend analysis — assoc_trend
text: data.table one row per exposure \(\times\) level \(\times\) model, containing: exposure Exposure variable name. level Factor level (ordered factor preserving original level order). term Coefficient name returned model (reference row uses paste0(exposure, ref_level)). model Ordered factor: Unadjusted < Age sex adjusted < Fully adjusted. n Participants model (NA removal). ... Effect estimate columns categorical model: HR//beta, CI_lower, CI_upper, p_value, formatted label. Reference row HR = 1 / beta = 0 NA CI p. Cox models additionally include n_events person_years; logistic includes n_cases; linear includes se. HR_per_score / OR_per_score / beta_per_score Per-score-unit effect estimate trend model. Shared across levels within exposure \(\times\) model. HR_per_score_label / OR_per_score_label / beta_per_score_label Formatted string per-score estimate. p_trend P-value linear trend trend model. Shared across levels within exposure \(\times\) model.

title: Dose-response trend analysis — assoc_trend
text: Categorical model - exposure treated factor; produces one row per non-reference level (HR / / \(\beta\) vs reference). Trend model - exposure recoded numeric scores (default: 0, 1, 2, ...); produces per-score-unit effect estimate (*_per_score) p_trend. results merged: output contains reference row (effect = 1 / 0, CI = NA) followed non-reference rows, *_per_score p_trend appended shared columns (value within exposure \(\times\) model combination). Scores: default levels scored 0, 1, 2, ... reference group = 0 step = 1 unit. Supply scores use meaningful units (e.g. median years per category) - p_trend per-score estimate change; per-category HRs unaffected. Adjustment models: follows logic assoc_coxph - Unadjusted Age--sex-adjusted models included default (base = TRUE); Fully adjusted model added covariates non-NULL.

text: chr Integer vector. Chromosomes process. Default: 1:22. dest Character scalar. RAP destination path output PGEN files. Default: "/pgen/". maf Numeric scalar. Minor allele frequency filter passed plink2 --maf. Variants MAF threshold excluded. Default: 0.01. Must (0, 0.5). instance Character scalar. Instance type preset: "standard" "large". See Details. Default: "standard". priority Character scalar. Job priority: "low" "high". Default: "low".

title: Convert UKB imputed BGEN files to PGEN on RAP — grs_bgen2pgen
text: character vector job IDs (one per chromosome), returned invisibly. Failed submissions NA. Use job_ls, job_status, job_wait monitor progress.

title: Convert UKB imputed BGEN files to PGEN on RAP — grs_bgen2pgen
text: function auto-generates plink2 driver script, uploads RAP project root (/) RAP, loops chr submitting one job per chromosome. 500 ms pause submissions prevents API rate-limiting. Output path critical. driver script writes plink2 output /home/dnanexus/// - fixed path Swiss Army Knife auto-uploads dest completion. Output files per chromosome: chr{N}_maf001.pgen/.pvar/.psam/.log. Instance types: "standard" mem2_ssd1_v2_x4: 4 cores, 12 GB RAM. Suitable smaller chromosomes (roughly chr 15–22). "large" mem2_ssd2_v2_x8: 8 cores, 28 GB RAM, 640 GB SSD. Required large chromosomes (roughly chr 1–14) standard storage insufficient.

title: Convert UKB imputed BGEN files to PGEN on RAP — grs_bgen2pgen

what: Check and export a GRS weights file — grs_check
title: Check and export a GRS weights file — grs_check

title: Check and export a GRS weights file — grs_check

title: Check and export a GRS weights file — grs_check
text: file Character scalar. Path input weights file. Read via data.table::fread (format auto-detected; handles CSV, TSV, space-delimited, etc.). dest Character scalar. Output path validated, space-delimited weights file. Default: "weights.txt".

title: Check and export a GRS weights file — grs_check
text: data.table columns snp, effect_allele, beta, returned invisibly.

title: Check and export a GRS weights file — grs_check
text: input file must contain least three columns (additional columns ignored): snp SNP identifier, expected rs + digits format. effect_allele Effect allele; must one / T / C / G. beta Effect size (log-beta coefficient); must numeric. Checks performed: Required columns present. NA values three required columns. duplicate snp identifiers. snp matches rs[0-9]+ pattern (warning ). effect_allele contains / T / C / G (warning ). beta numeric (error ).

what: Calculate genetic risk scores from PGEN files on RAP — grs_score
title: Calculate genetic risk scores from PGEN files on RAP — grs_score
text: Uploads local SNP weight files RAP project root, submits one Swiss Army Knife job per GRS. job runs plink2 --score across 22 chromosomes saves single CSV dest completion. Jobs run parallel; use job_ls monitor progress.

title: Calculate genetic risk scores from PGEN files on RAP — grs_score
code: grs_score(   file,   pgen_dir = "/mnt/project/pgen",   dest = "/grs/",   maf = 0.01,   instance = "standard",   priority = "low" )

title: Calculate genetic risk scores from PGEN files on RAP — grs_score
text: file Named character vector local weight file paths. Names become GRS identifiers (output column = GRS_<name>). Example: c(grs_a = "weights_a.txt"). pgen_dir Character scalar. Path PGEN files RAP. Default: "/mnt/project/pgen". dest Character scalar. RAP destination path output CSV files. Default: "/grs/". maf Numeric scalar. MAF filter threshold used locating PGEN files. Must match value used grs_bgen2pgen. Default: 0.01. instance Character scalar. Instance type preset: "standard" "large". Default: "standard". priority Character scalar. Job priority: "low" "high". Default: "low".

title: Calculate genetic risk scores from PGEN files on RAP — grs_score
text: named character vector job IDs (one per GRS), returned invisibly. Failed submissions NA. Use job_ls monitor progress.

title: Calculate genetic risk scores from PGEN files on RAP — grs_score
text: Weight files three columns (delimiter, header required): Column 1 Variant ID (e.g. rs IDs). Column 2 Effect allele (A1). Column 3 Effect weight (beta / log-). matches output format grs_check. Output per job: dest/<score_name>_scores.csv columns IID GRS score (named GRS_<name>). Instance types: "standard" mem2_ssd1_v2_x4: 4 cores, 12 GB RAM. "large" mem2_ssd2_v2_x8: 8 cores, 28 GB RAM.

title: Calculate genetic risk scores from PGEN files on RAP — grs_score

Validate GRS predictive performance — grs_validate
text: GRS column, computes four sets validation metrics: Per SD - (logistic) HR (Cox) per 1-SD increase. High vs Low - / HR comparing top 20\ (extreme tertile grouping: Low / Mid / High). Trend test - P-trend across quartiles (Q1–Q4). Discrimination - AUC (logistic) C-index (Cox).

title: Validate GRS predictive performance — grs_validate
code: grs_validate(   data,   grs_cols = NULL,   outcome_col,   time_col = NULL,   covariates = NULL )

title: Validate GRS predictive performance — grs_validate
text: data data.frame data.table. grs_cols Character vector GRS column names validate. NULL (default), columns whose names contain "grs" (case-insensitive) selected automatically. outcome_col Character scalar. Name outcome column (0/1 TRUE/FALSE). time_col Character scalar NULL. Name follow-time column. NULL (default), logistic regression used; supplied, Cox regression used. covariates Character vector NULL. Covariates fully adjusted model. NULL, unadjusted age-sex adjusted models run.

title: Validate GRS predictive performance — grs_validate
text: named list four data.table elements: per_sd: / HR per 1-SD increase GRS. high_vs_low: / HR High vs Low extreme tertile. trend: P-trend across Q1–Q4 quartiles. discrimination: AUC (logistic) C-index (Cox) 95\

title: Validate GRS predictive performance — grs_validate
text: GRS grouping columns created internally via derive_cut added user's data.  time_col NULL, logistic regression used throughout; supplied, Cox proportional hazards models used. Models follow adjustment logic assoc_logistic / assoc_coxph: unadjusted age-sex adjusted models always included; fully adjusted model added covariates non-NULL.

title: Validate GRS predictive performance — grs_validate
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Logistic (cross-sectional) res <- grs_validate(   data        = cohort,   grs_cols    = c("GRS_a_z", "GRS_b_z"),   outcome_col = "outcome" )  # Cox (survival) res <- grs_validate(   data        = cohort,   grs_cols    = c("GRS_a_z", "GRS_b_z"),   outcome_col = "outcome",   time_col    = "followup_years",   covariates  = c("age", "sex", paste0("pc", 1:10)) )  res$per_sd res$discrimination } # }
